exemplar.title: To what extent is Bahnhofsviertel suffering from urban stress?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is Bahnhofsviertel suffering from urban stress?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly geographical with two explicit, testable hypotheses linked to theory
Precise location (Bahnhofsviertel and Westend) justified by demographic and land-use data
Multiple maps at different scales with labeling and conventions provide clear spatial context
weaknesses: Some maps lack a scale bar and north arrow, reducing full cartographic clarity
Second hypothesis wording is incomplete on the page
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the highest band descriptors: the question is framed with theoretical links and hypotheses, the context is specific and feasible for fieldwork, and mapping conventions are largely followed. Minor omissions in map conventions and a truncated hypothesis do not undermine the overall excellence in this criterion.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Comprehensive methods table detailing equipment, procedures and justifications for all five variables
Clear sampling strategy with 15 evenly spaced sites aligned to the research question
Correct use of anemometer and thermometer yielding reliable primary data
weaknesses: Lacks details on timing control and pilot testing to ensure consistency
Visual estimation methods (e.g., storey counts) risk observer bias without calibration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although some procedural refinements (pilot studies, inter-observer checks) are absent, the methods are well described, justified and correctly implemented. The coherent alignment to the research question and the reliability of the data collected justify full marks for methods.

strengths: All datasets collected (density, temperature, wind, land use, pedestrian counts) are directly relevant to testing the hypotheses
Well-structured tables and an annotated photograph enhance data communication
Overall adherence to conventions with numbered figures, captions and consistent headings
weaknesses: Inconsistent use of symbols (Greek letters) in tables causes confusion
Some maps still lack scale bars and land-use tables are overly complex
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data are sufficient and pertinent, and presentation techniques are appropriate with mostly effective conventions. Occasional formatting errors and overly complex tables prevent top-band performance but place the work solidly at the upper end of band 3â4.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate descriptive statistics (averages, comparisons) used correctly across datasets
Trends and anomalies are identified and linked to Core Frame, UHI and Bid-Rent theory
Hypotheses are revisited with clear reference to findings
weaknesses: No inferential statistical tests or confidence intervals are attempted
Analysis of wind speed and pedestrian data lacks discussion of variability and outliers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis applies correct descriptive and some statistical techniques, identifies patterns and anomalies, and links results back to theory. While gaps remain (no inferential tests, some variables less deeply explored), the work exceeds basic descriptive levels and fits the 5â6 band at the lower end.

strengths: Conclusion clearly states support for both hypotheses with specific evidence (e.g., temperature change, storey differences)
Links back explicitly to urban stress concepts discussed earlier
weaknesses: Second hypothesis statement is cut off, preventing full closure
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully supported by the analysis and directly addresses the research question, satisfying the top band descriptor for this criterion despite a minor omission in the text.

strengths: Identifies key limitations (temporal variation, traffic count scope, observer bias) and acknowledges their impact on data reliability
Offers relevant suggestions for repeat measurements and standardized observation
weaknesses: Suggestions lack detailed impact analysis (e.g., quantified reliability gains) and deeper reflection on alternative methodologies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation outlines pertinent strengths and weaknesses along with meaningful improvements, meeting the descriptor for band 2. It falls short of the depth and comprehensive impact analysis required for the top band.

strandSelections.reasoning: Multiple maps move from European to city scale, include a compass rose, route line and labelled sampling sites. Although a scale bar is not visible on every map, overall the mapping is clear, follows most conventions and allows the reader to visualise spatial patterns, fitting the highest band.

The research question is clearly geographical, focused on the spatial extent of urban stress in a defined district, and directly linked to syllabus ideas of urban stress, micro-climates, the urban heat-island effect and bid-rent theory. Two explicit hypotheses are stated, showing that the question has been framed so that it can be tested with primary data. This satisfies the descriptor for the top band.

Information is presented mainly through well-structured data tables and one annotated photograph. While tables are appropriate, there is a lack of more powerful graphical displays (scatter graphs, choropleths) that would maximise communication. Thus techniques are appropriate but not the most effective, warranting the 3-4 band with a score of 3.

Techniques were applied correctly (e.g., two trials for wind and temperature, use of anemometer and thermometer) and yielded primary data across 15 sites, but reliability issues (acknowledged temporal variation, visual estimation of storeys) reduce confidence. The quantity of data is more than minimal, so band 2 is appropriate.

The student calculates averages and compares values across sites, which are appropriate descriptive statistics. No inferential tests or confidence limits are attempted, so the work does not reach bands 5-6 or 7-8, but the descriptive treatment is correct, fitting the 3-4 band at the lower score.

Trends (decrease in building density, unexpected temperature pattern) and anomalies are identified and linked to Core Frame, Bid-Rent and UHI theory, showing clear interpretation. However, discussion is brief and not fully comprehensive, so the mid band (3-4) with 3 marks is justified.

The student locates the study precisely (Bahnhofsviertel and Westend in Frankfurt) and justifies choice through description of demographics, land use, crime and transport links. Fifteen numbered sampling points are identified, demonstrating feasibility for primary data collection. Therefore the location specificity and geographic context meet band 3 expectations.

The evaluation identifies key limitations (temporal variation, single-day traffic counts, visual estimation bias) and briefly comments on their impact. While relevant, the depth is moderate rather than comprehensive, corresponding to band 2.

All datasets collected (building density, temperature, wind, land-use, pedestrian counts) are directly relevant to assessing urban stress and generally sufficient for two hypotheses. Some gaps exist (pollution not measured despite being mentioned), so the strand fits the lower end of the 3-4 band.

The conclusion restates outcomes for both hypotheses and cites evidence (difference in storeys, +2.5 Â°C change). Links back to urban stress are made, showing support by analysis, so the higher of the two bands (2 marks) is earned.

Both hypotheses are revisited, and each variable is discussed, yet wind speed, land-use and pedestrian data receive minimal analytical depth, leaving evident gaps. Thus analysis is adequate but incomplete, fitting the 3-4 band at 3.

A methods table states equipment, description and justification for five variables, and basic sampling strategy (15 evenly spaced sites) is outlined. However, detail on timing control, pilot studies or theoretical alignment of each technique is limited, so the explanation is adequate rather than comprehensive, matching band 2.

Figures are numbered and captioned; tables have headings; maps contain a north arrow. Nevertheless, scale bars are missing on some maps and unusual symbols appear in some tables (Greek letters), showing occasional errors rather than frequent ones. This aligns with the middle band (3-4) at score 3.

Suggestions are provided (repeat measurements at fixed times, use extended periods, maintain same observer) and are relevant, but explanations of how each change would improve reliability remain surface-level. This meets the descriptor for band 2 rather than the detailed expectations of band 3.

annotations.comment: Context description of sampling districts is thorough, using demographics and crime data to justify site selection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1 map presents multi-scale context clearly with labeled sites, aiding reader orientation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Some maps lack a north arrow and scale bar; ensure all spatial representations follow cartographic conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The methods table describes equipment and justification but omits details on timing control and pilot testing.
Visual observation methods (e.g., building storey count) risk observer bias; include calibration or inter-observer checks.
The research question is clearly geographical, linking urban stress to microclimate and bid-rent theory with testable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The introductory map lacks a scale bar, reducing clarity of spatial extentâadd scale bars to all maps.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Definitions of urban stress and microclimate are detailed but would benefit from direct links to geographical theory.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The second hypothesis text is cut off, leaving the investigative focus incomplete. Clarify wording to ensure full hypothesis formulation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Recommendations to repeat measurements are relevant; extend by specifying frequency and expected reliability gains.
Correlation between building density and UHI effect is stated but would be stronger with quantitative theoretical justification.
The building density table uses Greek symbols inconsistently; replace with clear numeric entries and correct legend.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Interpretation notes effectively describe decreasing building density trends linked to theory.
Wind speed table does not address trial variability or outliers; discuss data cleaning or outlier treatment.
Wind speed interpretation links patterns to built environment and wind-tunneling, showing clear theoretical application.
Temperature measurement would gain reliability from use of continuous data loggers rather than two discrete trials.
Temperature table is well formatted and provides clear averages, facilitating comparison across sites.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Annotated photograph effectively illustrates urban stress factors and reinforces the Core Frame Model discussion.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Photograph interpretation is narrative; consider quantifying observed stressors (e.g., count litter items) for rigor.
Land use survey description is clear with defined categories, supporting relevance to urban stress factors.
Land use table is overly complex with cryptic symbols; simplify presentation or accompany with a chart.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Summary of land use trends links patterns to distance from the CBD, reflecting effective interpretation.
Pedestrian count method conflates vehicles and pedestrians; clarify classification and rationale for each metric.
Pedestrian count table uses Greek letters in counts, causing confusionâuse consistent numeric formatting.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Pedestrian interpretation insightfully links highest footfall to transit hub, demonstrating contextual understanding.
Hypothesis discussions integrate Core Frame and UHI theory, showing linkage of findings to geographic models.
The conclusion clearly states support for H1 with evidence, fulfilling strand requirements.
Second hypothesis conclusion is cut off mid-sentence, leaving reader without full closureâcomplete text.
Evaluation identifies key limitations and strengths but lacks deeper impact analysis of suggested improvements.
Bibliography entries show inconsistent MLA formatting; standardize punctuation and order of information.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
